Rule: Hierarchy must follow F-pattern for text-heavy pages.
Keep micro-interactions under 200ms to avoid sluggishness.
Contrast ratio must be 4.5:1 for standard text (3:1 for large text 18px+).
Group related fields and use vertical stacking.
Use a scale with consistent steps (100, 200, 300...) for layering.
Confirm actions with toast or inline success states — never leave users guessing.
Primary buttons should have distinct visual weight; only one primary CTA per screen.
Line height should be 1.5x font size for body text; 1.2x for headlines.
Use Z-pattern for pages with limited text and strong visual CTAs.
Use 8pt increments for all spacing and sizing to maintain visual rhythm.
Group related elements together; use whitespace to separate unrelated content.
Keep components visually and behaviorally identical across all screens and pages.
Reduce choices per screen (Hick's Law): more options = longer decision time. Aim for 5–7 max.
Make tap/click targets large enough for their importance (Fitts's Law). Min 44x44pt on mobile, 32x32px on web.
Design for recognition not recall — show options, labels, and icons rather than requiring memorization.
Show system status at all times: loading indicators for >300ms operations; progress bars for >2s operations.
Error messages must say what went wrong, why, and exactly how to fix it. Never just 'Error occurred'.
Keep onboarding to 3 screens or fewer. Use progressive disclosure — teach by doing not by explaining.
Minimum touch target size: 44x44pt iOS, 48x48dp Android. Never smaller.
Place primary navigation in the thumb-friendly zone (bottom 40% of screen on mobile).
Limit bottom navigation to 3–5 items. Use a drawer for secondary/infrequent items.
Use skeleton screens instead of spinners for content-heavy loads. Skeleton screens reduce perceived wait time by 20–30%.
Infinite scroll is suitable for content feeds. Use pagination for task-completion interfaces (search results
Use the 60-30-10 rule: 60% primary/neutral, 30% secondary, 10% accent.
Never use color as the only differentiator for states or data — always pair with icon or label for accessibility.
Dark mode: pure black (#000000) only on OLED. Use #121212 or #1A1A1A for most dark surfaces.
Whitespace is not empty space — it is a design element. Increase whitespace around key CTAs to improve click-through.
Limit a design to 2 font families maximum. Use weight and size variation to create hierarchy within a family.
Body text minimum: 16px on web, 14pt on mobile. Never go smaller for paragraph content.
CTA buttons must contrast sharply with their background. Use action verbs: 'Start Free Trial' not 'Submit'.
Repeat the primary CTA at least twice on long pages — above the fold and after key social proof.
Apply Gestalt principles: similarity, proximity, continuity, and closure guide visual grouping before any label does.
All interactive elements must have visible focus states — outline: 2px solid primary-color; outline-offset: 2px.
Every image needs an alt attribute. Decorative images use alt='' to be ignored by screen readers.
All form inputs need associated <label> elements — never rely on placeholder text as a label.
Modals must trap focus inside, restore focus on close, and dismiss on Escape key press.
Provide a skip-to-main-content link as the first focusable element on every page.
Always wrap animations in @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) and provide static alternatives.
Page transitions: 200–400ms ease-out. Entry animations: 150–300ms. Exit animations: 100–200ms.
Never animate more than 2–3 CSS properties simultaneously — GPU handles transform and opacity best.
Animations should serve a purpose: guide attention or communicate state change. Decorative-only motion adds cognitive load.
Never leave an empty state blank. Use a helpful message, illustration, and a clear next action.
Empty state copy must be human: 'No messages yet — start a conversation!' not 'No data available'.
Modals are for urgent or critical information only. Never use modals for non-blocking content — use drawers or inline.
Always provide a clear visible close button on modals. Never require pressing Escape as the only close method.
Navigation should answer 3 questions instantly: Where am I? Where can I go? How do I get back?
Never hide high-frequency actions in hamburger menus on mobile. Reserve hamburgers for secondary, infrequent items.
Breadcrumbs are required on sites with 3+ hierarchy levels. They reduce navigation errors by up to 39%.
Validate inputs inline on blur (when user leaves the field) — never wait until form submission to show all errors.
For long forms, show a progress bar and split into logical steps. Never show a form with more than 7 fields at once.
Auto-focus the first input field when a form opens. Provide sensible tab order (top-to-bottom left-to-right).
Use smart defaults wherever possible — pre-fill known information, suggest the most likely option.
Destructive actions (delete, remove) require confirmation — a dialog with the consequence clearly stated.
Show immediate feedback within 100ms of any user action — even a micro-animation signals the system heard the user.
For operations over 10 seconds, show a progress percentage or step count. Never show an infinite spinner for long tasks.
Lazy-load images and off-screen content. Use Intersection Observer, not scroll event listeners.
Perceived performance matters more than actual performance. Preload the next screen's data before the user navigates to it.
Target Core Web Vitals: LCP < 2.5s, INP < 200ms, CLS < 0.1. These directly correlate with conversion and retention.
Never use dark patterns. Specifically avoid: roach motel (easy in, hard out), confirmshaming, hidden costs, pre-ticked boxes.
Cancellation must be as easy as signup. A cancel button must be accessible in 2 clicks or fewer.
Do not use artificial urgency or false scarcity (fake countdown timers or 'only 2 left' when untrue).
Display trust signals near conversion points: security badges, testimonials, and money-back guarantees.
Social proof is most effective near the CTA. Place review counts, logos, or user numbers directly above or below the primary action.
The Aha Moment is the goal of onboarding. Every step must move the user closer to experiencing core value.
Delay registration walls — let users experience value first. Apps that do this see up to 50% higher activation rates.
Provide a progress indicator for multi-step onboarding flows. Users who see progress are 40% more likely to complete.
Cognitive load must be minimized. One primary action per screen. Remove every element that does not help the user complete their current task.
Apply progressive disclosure: show only what users need right now. Reveal complexity on demand, not upfront.
Personalize interfaces using behavioral data: show recently used features, remember preferences, adapt content to context.
Design mobile-first. Define breakpoints at 375px, 768px, 1024px, and 1440px. Test on real devices at every breakpoint.
Never use fixed pixel widths for layout containers. Use fluid grids with max-width constraints.
Never use emojis as functional UI icons. Use SVG icon libraries (Heroicons
Icon-only buttons must have an aria-label and ideally a tooltip on hover. Never icon-only for critical actions.
Cards must have clearly defined boundaries (shadow, border, or background contrast) to signal interactivity.
Clickable cards need a visible hover state — background change, shadow elevation, or border highlight.
For data tables: freeze the header row on scroll, allow column sorting, and provide row hover highlighting.
Align numbers right-justified in tables so decimal points line up for easy scanning.
Search inputs should support instant results (< 300ms debounce). Show helpful empty-state when no results are found.
Forgiving search: handle typos, synonyms, and partial matches. Never return zero results without a suggestion.
Notifications must be dismissible and time-limited. Auto-dismiss success toasts after 3–5s. Error toasts persist until dismissed.
Limit notification types to 3: success (green) / warning (amber) / error (red). Never use 6+ color-coded states.
Establish a spacing scale (4px base): 4 / 8 / 12 / 16 / 24 / 32 / 48 / 64 / 96. Never use arbitrary values.
Increase internal padding before increasing external margin — content inside a component needs breathing room first.
Use elevation (shadow intensity) to communicate layer depth. Higher elevation = more important / closer to user.
Consistent shadow scale: Level 1 (cards), Level 2 (dropdowns), Level 3 (modals), Level 4 (popovers).
UI copy must be scannable: short sentences, active voice, second person ('You', 'Your'). Never passive or corporate.
Button labels must be action verbs describing the outcome. 'Save Changes' not 'OK'. 'Delete Account' not 'Confirm'.
Microcopy at friction points (payment, deletion, permissions) must be reassuring and specific about consequences.
Support keyboard-only navigation throughout. Tab order must follow visual reading order (top-left to bottom-right).
ARIA roles should complement semantic HTML, not replace it. Use <button> for buttons, not <div role='button'>.
On iOS, follow Human Interface Guidelines. On Android, follow Material Design 3. Never mix platform conventions in native apps.
Respect platform-specific gestures: iOS swipe-left edge to go back, Android system back button. Never fight native conventions.
For information-dense UIs (dashboards, admin): use compact spacing with clear row dividers. For consumer: use generous whitespace.
Test designs with real users at every major milestone. A design not tested with users is a guess, not a decision.
5 users can reveal 85% of usability issues. Run 5-participant tests iteratively rather than one large study.
Apply the 80/20 rule: 20% of UI changes deliver 80% of UX improvement. Focus on the highest-friction touchpoints first.
Design systems prevent inconsistency. Every team building without a shared component library will ship different UX.
